retrieved_docs.title: Langfuse: Open-Source LLM Observability and Evaluation
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Langfuse: Open-Source LLM Observability and Evaluation

Langfuse is an open-source platform for tracing, evaluating, and monitoring LLM applications. It captures full traces of agent runs including prompts, completions, tool calls, and latency. Langfuse integrates with LangChain, LlamaIndex, and OpenAI SDK, and supports human annotation workflows for building evaluation datasets. It is used by teams to detect regressions, measure hallucination rates, and optimize prompt quality.

retrieved_docs.title: Cursor Blog — cursorbench
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Cursor Blog — cursorbench

How we compare model quality in Cursor
Developers are asking coding agents to take on longer, more complex tasks that span multiple files, tools, and steps. As these requests grow in scope, the evals that measure agent performance need to evolve with them.
At Cursor, we use a hybrid online-offline eval process to keep our understanding of model quality aligned with what developers actually do.
The offline part uses CursorBench, our internal eval suite based on real Cursor sessions from our engineering team. Because tasks come from actual Cursor usage rather than public repositories, CursorBench is both better at distinguishing between models and more aligned with real developer outcomes than public benchmarks.
We built CursorBench to measure multiple dimensions of agent performance including solution correctness, code quality, efficiency, and interaction behavior. This blog focuses on solution correctness results, but in practice we evaluate agents across all of these axes.
We supplement CursorBench with controlled analysis on live traffic. These online evals catch regressions that offline suites miss, like where the agent's output looks correct to a grader but feels worse to a dev

retrieved_docs.title: Braintrust: Enterprise LLM Evaluation Platform
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Braintrust: Enterprise LLM Evaluation Platform

Braintrust is an enterprise-grade evaluation platform for LLM applications that supports automated scoring, human review workflows, and regression tracking across model versions. Teams use Braintrust to define evaluation datasets, run scoring functions (LLM-as-judge, exact match, semantic similarity), and track quality metrics over time. It competes with Langfuse in the evaluation-native tooling space.

retrieved_docs.title: YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure
retrieved_docs.snippet:
YC W25 Request for Startups: AI Infrastructure

Y Combinator's Winter 2025 Request for Startups highlights AI infrastructure as a top investment thesis. Key areas include: developer tools for agentic AI (debugging, testing, deployment), evaluation frameworks for LLM reliability, multi-modal data pipelines, and cost-optimization layers for large language model inference. YC notes that startups solving agent observability and grounding are especially well-positioned.

retrieved_docs.title: HuggingFace Blog — Eval Costs Bottleneck
retrieved_docs.snippet:
HuggingFace Blog — Eval Costs Bottleneck

AI evals are becoming the new compute bottleneck
Summary. AI evaluation has crossed a cost threshold that changes who can do it. The Holistic Agent Leaderboard (HAL) recently spent about $40,000 to run 21,730 agent rollouts across 9 models and 9 benchmarks. A single GAIA run on a frontier model can cost $2,829 before caching. Exgentic's $22,000 sweep across agent configurations found a 33× cost spread on identical tasks, isolating scaffold choice as a first-order cost driver, and UK-AISI recently scaled agentic steps into the millions to study inference-time compute. In scientific ML, The Well costs about 960 H100-hours to evaluate one new architecture and 3,840 H100-hours for a full four-baseline sweep. While compression techniques have been proposed for static benchmarks, new agent benchmarks are noisy, scaffold-sensitive, and only partly compressible. Training-in-the-loop benchmarks are expensive by construction, and when you try to add reliability to these evals, repeated runs further multiply the cost.
Making static LLM benchmarks cheaper
The cost problem started before agents. When Stanford's CRFM released HELM in 2022, the paper's own per-model accounting showed API costs ra

retrieved_docs.title: Weaviate Blog — Concepts
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Weaviate Blog — Concepts

Your LLM Is Only as Good as What It Retrieves
A Researcher's Perspective on Retrieval Quality in RAG Systems
A Researcher's Perspective on Retrieval Quality in RAG Systems
A deep dive into Engram, our managed memory service for agents which is simple to get started but adaptable to any use case.
Two weeks of dogfooding Engram, Weaviate's memory product, in daily Claude Code sessions. This surfaced where a dedicated memory product adds value, and the specific mechanics that prevent integration with coding assistants from working well.
Memory isn't just a feature for AI applications—it's infrastructure. As agents scale, the limited loop of stateless interactions breaks down, and continuity becomes a systems problem that requires active maintenance.
Context engineering is how AI agents manage LLM memory—selecting, retrieving, and organizing context from short-term and long-term memory to improve reliability in production.

retrieved_docs.title: Galileo — www.rungalileo.io
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Galileo — www.rungalileo.io

Don't just monitor AI failures. Stop them.
Galileo is the AI observability and eval engineering platform where offline evals become production guardrails.
Capture your groundtruth
Build your datasets from synthetic, development, and live production data. Capture subject matter expert annotations to create a living asset that continuously grounds your AI systems.
Build accurate evals
Don't settle for generic evals with less than 70% F1 scores. Galileo auto-tunes metrics from live feedback to create evals that are fit to your environments.
Go from evals to guardrails
Today’s evals are tomorrow’s guardrails. But only if you can run them at scale. Distill your optimized evals into Luna models that monitor 100% of your traffic at 97% lower cost.
Solve the AI measurement problem
You can't ship when you're flying blind. Start with 20+ out-of-box evals for RAG, agents, safety, and security—then build the custom evaluators to encode your domain expertise. Only Galileo distills expensive LLM-as-judge evaluators into compact Luna models that run with low-latency and low-cost.
Accelerate your deployments
Developers need to know what to fix. So Galileo's insights engine analyzes agent behavior

retrieved_docs.title: Composio: Tool Integration Layer for AI Agents
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Composio: Tool Integration Layer for AI Agents

Composio provides a managed integration layer that connects LLM agents to over 250 external tools and APIs (GitHub, Slack, Notion, Jira, etc.) with built-in authentication handling. It simplifies the tool-use layer of agentic pipelines, letting developers focus on agent logic rather than API plumbing. Composio integrates natively with CrewAI, LangChain, and AutoGen.

retrieved_docs.title: LangGraph: Stateful Agent Orchestration with Graphs
retrieved_docs.snippet:
LangGraph: Stateful Agent Orchestration with Graphs

LangGraph, built on top of LangChain, provides a graph-based abstraction for building reliable, stateful LLM agents. Developers define nodes (agent steps) and edges (transitions) in a directed graph, enabling cyclic workflows, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and fine-grained state management. LangGraph is widely used for production-grade agentic pipelines requiring persistence and controllability.

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025
retrieved_docs.snippet:
Latent Space: The Rise of Agentic AI Platforms in 2025

The Latent Space newsletter surveys the agentic AI platform landscape as of mid-2025. The analysis identifies three dominant approaches: (1) orchestration frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph) focused on multi-agent coordination; (2) RAG-first platforms (LlamaIndex, Vertex AI Search) focused on grounding agents in enterprise data; and (3) evaluation-native platforms (Langfuse, Braintrust, PromptLayer) focused on reliability and measurement. The newsletter argues that evaluation will become the key differentiator as models commoditize.
